content.text: Disclosure timeline management is the process of setting, tracking, and enforcing the time window between a researcher's initial vulnerability report and the public disclosure of that vulnerability and its fix. The 90-day coordinated disclosure window has become an industry standard established by Google Project Zero and widely adopted by major technology organizations. Remediation is the technical process of developing, testing, deploying, and verifying the fix within that window.

For AI-specific vulnerabilities, remediation is often more complex than for traditional software bugs. A prompt injection vulnerability may require changes at multiple layers: input sanitization in the application layer, system-prompt hardening, output monitoring, and potentially model fine-tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback to reduce susceptibility. Each layer has its own development and deployment timeline, and coordinating them within 90 days requires explicit program management.

The disclosure timeline is the axis around which the entire VDP relationship turns. A well-managed timeline builds researcher trust and user protection simultaneously: users are protected because the fix is deployed before the vulnerability details are public, and researchers are respected because their work is acknowledged within a predictable window. A poorly managed timeline results in one of two failure modes: premature unilateral disclosure by a frustrated researcher, or an indefinite fix delay that leaves users exposed while the vendor buys time.

Regulators are also beginning to examine disclosure timelines. The EU AI Act's provisions on post-market monitoring imply that deployers of high-risk AI systems must have processes for identifying and remediating significant issues in a timely way. A documented disclosure timeline framework with SLAs by severity level is evidence of that process.
Provides a predictable framework that researchers can plan their disclosure around, reducing the risk of premature publication
Creates a forcing function for engineering teams by establishing a hard deadline with organizational visibility
Ensures that users are protected by a deployed fix before the vulnerability details become publicly exploitable
Builds institutional knowledge about AI vulnerability remediation timelines that improves future planning
Documents the organization's response timeline for regulatory or legal purposes
Coordinates downstream communications: security advisories, customer notifications, press responses, and regulatory notifications all depend on the disclosure date
90 days is the industry default but may be insufficient for complex AI vulnerabilities requiring model retraining; shorter windows create more pressure but produce faster fixes on average
Disclosing immediately after a patch is ready but before broad deployment exposes users who have not yet received the patch
Coordinated disclosure that includes a joint advisory with the researcher produces more accurate public accounts but requires researcher cooperation
Assigning a CVE early creates a public record but also flags to other researchers and threat actors that a vulnerability exists; some vendors prefer to assign at the time of disclosure

The 90-day window runs from the date of the organization's initial triage acknowledgment, not from the date of the researcher's report. The window is divided into four operational phases: triage and validation, remediation, deployment and verification, and disclosure. Each phase has a target duration and a set of outputs.

The first phase confirms that the vulnerability is real, in scope, and accurately characterized. The security team reproduces the issue, assesses the impact, and scores the severity. For AI vulnerabilities, this phase often requires involvement from the AI engineering team to understand the model-layer dynamics. At the end of this phase, the engineering team has a validated problem statement and has begun scoping the fix.

The engineering team develops the fix. For application-layer issues such as prompt injection defenses, this may be a standard software engineering task. For model-layer issues such as jailbreak susceptibility or training data leakage, remediation may involve changes to system prompts, retrieval filters, output monitors, or a new model version. The fix is tested on a staging environment that the researcher may access to validate the remediation.

The fix is deployed to production and verified to be effective. For AI serving infrastructure, this includes confirming that the fix applies to all serving instances and model versions in use, not just the primary endpoint. The researcher is notified that the fix has been deployed and given an opportunity to verify it against their reproduction case in the production environment.

The security advisory is drafted, CVE is assigned, and the researcher reviews the draft for accuracy. On the agreed disclosure date, the advisory is published, the CVE record is updated, the researcher is credited, and any applicable bounty is paid. The researcher may publish their technical writeup simultaneously or after the advisory.

If the fix will not be deployed by day 80, the security team contacts the researcher before day 80 to request an extension. Extensions should be specific: a defined number of additional days with a specific technical justification. Extensions of more than 30 days are exceptional and require strong justification. If the researcher declines the extension, the organization should prepare for unilateral disclosure and issue a security advisory even if the fix is not complete, advising users on mitigations they can apply themselves.

The diagram shows the 90-day timeline divided into four phases, three disclosure outcomes (coordinated, extension, unilateral), and the patch-deploy-disclose sequence that governs the ordering of technical and communication activities.
Immediately when a valid vulnerability report is received: the clock starts at triage acknowledgment
When planning internal fix timelines: work backward from the disclosure deadline to set engineering milestones
When a fix is delayed: assess whether an extension is warranted and contact the researcher before the window expires
When a researcher threatens early disclosure: use the timeline framework to demonstrate the specific remaining steps and their durations
When disclosing without researcher coordination because a researcher is unresponsive: document the outreach attempts and proceed with the standard timeline
Document your standard timelines by severity

Define the target remediation timeline for each severity level and publish it in your VDP policy. Standard timelines: critical vulnerabilities within 30 days, high within 60 days, medium within 90 days, low on a best-effort basis. For AI vulnerabilities requiring model changes, define a separate extended timeline category with a maximum of 120 days, applicable only when approved by the CISO.

For each accepted vulnerability, create a disclosure calendar that shows the target dates for each phase: triage complete, fix development start, staging deployment, production deployment, advisory draft sent to researcher, disclosure date. Share this calendar with the researcher at triage completion so expectations are aligned from the start.
Set up engineering milestones in your project management system

Create a project or ticket in your engineering project management system for each accepted vulnerability with the disclosure deadline as a hard constraint. Use milestone alerts to notify the engineering lead when 30 days remain and when 14 days remain, triggering an escalation assessment.

Define what the organization does if a vulnerability is actively being exploited before the fix is ready. In this scenario, the standard coordinated window does not apply: the organization must immediately disclose the vulnerability with whatever mitigations are available, even if the full fix is not yet deployed. Document this escalation path so it is not improvised during a crisis.

# Disclosure calendar template for an accepted AI vulnerability

case_id: 2025-AI-042
title: "Indirect prompt injection via retrieved web content"
severity: high
received_date: 2025-07-15
triage_ack_date: 2025-07-15         # Day 0 — clock starts here
coordinated_window_days: 90
target_disclosure_date: 2025-10-13  # Day 90

milestones:
  triage_complete:
    target: 2025-07-25              # Day 10
    owner: ai_security_team
    output: validated reproduction case + severity score

  fix_development_start:
    target: 2025-07-28              # Day 13
    owner: engineering_team
    output: fix design document reviewed by researcher

  staging_deployment:
    target: 2025-08-25              # Day 41
    owner: engineering_team
    output: fix deployed to staging; researcher notified for validation

  researcher_validation:
    target: 2025-09-01              # Day 48
    owner: researcher
    output: researcher confirms fix addresses reproduction case

  production_deployment:
    target: 2025-09-22              # Day 69
    owner: engineering_team
    output: fix live on all production endpoints; verified effective

  advisory_draft_to_researcher:
    target: 2025-09-29              # Day 76
    owner: security_team
    output: researcher reviews and approves advisory text

  disclosure:
    target: 2025-10-13              # Day 90
    actions:
      - publish security advisory at security.example.com
      - update CVE record with full details
      - pay researcher bounty
      - researcher publishes technical writeup (coordinated)

## Emergency Disclosure Protocol (Active Exploitation)

Trigger: Credible evidence that the vulnerability is being actively exploited
         in the wild before the coordinated disclosure date.

Immediate actions (within 4 hours of confirmation):
1. CISO declares emergency disclosure; standard window suspended
2. Legal notified; regulatory notification assessment initiated
3. Researcher contacted immediately; coordination window cancelled with explanation
4. Engineering escalates to emergency fix track (parallel to disclosure)
5. Security advisory issued with current mitigation guidance even if patch
   is not yet deployed; updated when patch is available

Advisory content under active exploitation:
- Vulnerability description (sufficient for defenders to detect exploitation)
- Indicators of compromise if known
- Mitigations users can apply while patch is in development
- Expected patch availability timeline

Researcher handling under active exploitation:
- Inform researcher immediately; they are no longer bound by the embargo
- Offer to credit them in the advisory even though coordinated disclosure
  did not complete as planned
- If researcher is the source of active exploitation, escalate to Legal
  for CFAA / safe harbor assessment

Google Project Zero established the 90-day standard and has published extensively about cases where this timeline played out in practice. In one notable case involving a major AI inference provider, Project Zero researchers identified a critical vulnerability in the model serving infrastructure that could allow cross-customer context leakage. The vendor received the report, acknowledged within 24 hours, and provided weekly status updates throughout the 90-day window.

At day 75, the engineering team realized the fix required a refactor of the serving infrastructure that would take an additional 45 days. They contacted the Project Zero team, explained the technical complexity, and requested a 45-day extension. Project Zero declined but offered a 21-day extension based on the specific technical justification provided. The vendor accepted, deployed a partial mitigation at day 90 that reduced but did not eliminate the risk, and completed the full fix by day 111. The joint advisory credited Project Zero researchers and acknowledged the extended timeline with a technical explanation.
Starting the clock from the researcher's report date rather than the triage acknowledgment date:
The clock should start when the organization confirms receipt and begins the triage process, not from when the researcher sent the email
Not publishing the timeline in the VDP policy:
Researchers who do not know the expected timeline cannot plan around it; some will set their own deadlines unilaterally
Disclosing before the patch is deployed to all affected systems:
Disclosing a vulnerability while even a subset of production instances remain unpatched creates a window of active exploitation
Failing to notify the researcher before requesting an extension:
Contacting the researcher on day 89 to request an extension gives them no reasonable time to assess the request and is often interpreted as bad faith
Organizations without a documented emergency protocol improvise under pressure, producing inconsistent and often inadequate responses to active exploitation
Define and publish severity-specific timelines in the VDP policy so researchers can plan around them before submitting reports
Share a disclosure calendar with the researcher at triage completion so both parties have the same milestone expectations
Deploy the fix to all production instances before the public disclosure date; partial deployment creates an active exploitation window
Contact the researcher at least 14 days before the disclosure deadline if an extension is needed; do not wait until the deadline
Have a documented emergency disclosure protocol for active exploitation scenarios that does not depend on completing the coordinated window
Credit the researcher in the advisory and update the CVE record regardless of whether the disclosure was coordinated or unilateral
Review timelines retrospectively after each closure to improve future planning; track actual vs. target timelines by severity and vulnerability class

Managing disclosure timelines across multiple concurrent vulnerability reports requires a tracking system. At low volume, a spreadsheet can suffice. At medium volume, a project management tool with milestone alerting is essential. At high volume, a dedicated VDP platform with built-in SLA tracking is necessary to prevent timeline slippage going undetected.

The most common bottleneck is engineering capacity: the team responsible for fixing vulnerabilities has other priorities, and without explicit milestone alerting and escalation paths, disclosure deadlines can approach without the fix being ready. Establish clear escalation triggers that bring the CISO and engineering leadership into visibility when a milestone is missed.
HackerOne, Bugcrowd, and Intigriti provide built-in SLA tracking with visual dashboards showing each report's position in the disclosure timeline
Jira, Linear, or GitHub Issues with milestone dates and automated alerts for approaching deadlines
NVD CVE database; register as a CNA (CVE Numbering Authority) to self-assign CVEs for faster issuance
AI-specific attack techniques are being added to MITRE ATT&CK; use the framework to categorize disclosed vulnerabilities consistently
Publish remediated vulnerabilities in the National Vulnerability Database for downstream security tooling that uses NVD feeds for patch management

The disclosure timeline management process is itself a security-sensitive operation. The list of accepted but undisclosed vulnerabilities is a high-value target: anyone who knows which AI vulnerabilities exist but are not yet patched can exploit them before the fix is deployed. Access to the vulnerability tracking system must be strictly controlled, limited to the personnel directly involved in triage, remediation, and disclosure.

Reliability of the process matters because a missed disclosure deadline has concrete consequences. Set up monitoring on the disclosure tracking system itself: alert when a report is approaching its deadline with no fix in production, and escalate automatically when a deadline is breached without a researcher-agreed extension.
The 90-day coordinated disclosure window is the industry standard; define shorter windows for critical severity and a separate extended category for complex AI remediation
Share a disclosure calendar with the researcher at triage completion so both parties have the same milestone expectations from the start
The patch-deploy-disclose sequence is non-negotiable: the fix must be deployed to all affected production instances before vulnerability details are made public
Contact the researcher at least 14 days before the deadline to request an extension; a last-minute request is interpreted as bad faith
Define and document an emergency disclosure protocol for active exploitation that does not depend on completing the coordinated window
Credit the researcher in the advisory and update the CVE record regardless of whether the disclosure was fully coordinated
Access to the undisclosed vulnerability list must be strictly controlled; it is a high-value target for threat actors
